properties.name: fitness_approach2_w01_data
properties.description: Fitness sensitivity approach 2 w=0.1 (1/5 success)
Entropy fitness, minimal terminals {k,1,-1,2}, no injection: 5/5 seeds found Leibniz
GP convergence fitness, minimal terminals, no injection, pop=1000: 2/5 seeds found Leibniz
GP convergence fitness, minimal terminals, no injection, pop=2000: 5/5 seeds. Phase transition at pop=2000.
Entropy v3 minimal total runtime for 5/5 discovery (all seeds)
Constant information gain rate for all discovered Leibniz expressions. Theoretical rate from error bound 1/(2T+1).
Entropy fitness fails at 15 terminals (0/5). Wrong-limit attractor 5/((6+4k)(k-2)) scores ti=15.93 > Leibniz 15.29
Wrong-limit attractor 5/((6+4k)(k-2)) achieves 15.93 bits at T=10000, exceeding Leibniz (15.29). Converges to ~0.7855 != pi/4.
Leibniz series info(T=10000) = -log2(|error|) = 15.29 bits
GP v3 minimal with alpha=0.5 (convergence bonus weight): 4/5 seeds found Leibniz
Fitness sensitivity: large-T penalty w=0.1 on 15-terminal entropy: 1/5. Only fix that found Leibniz (seed 137, 389 gens)
GP v2 and Entropy v2 5/5 results were CONFOUNDED: Leibniz tree injected at gen 0, survived via elitism. Not discovered.
Implement operations-per-bit efficiency fitness: info_gain / tree_complexity. Test on 15-terminal problem.
Formal analysis: wrong-limit attractor density as function of terminal set size N. Combinatorial argument for why 4→5/5 and 15→0/5.
Formalize second-order kinetics interpretation: Leibniz error 1/(2T+1) as second-order rate law. Prove uniqueness of constant info rate under parsimony.
Vector space decomposition of fitness landscape. Min-gradient energy dispersion analysis (in progress).
Run entropy stress test levels L2-L4 (42, 44, 41 terminals). Stopped at L1=0/5 but may be informative with fitness fixes.
Test combined optimal config: pop=2000, alpha=0.5, entropy fitness, on 15-terminal set. Never tested — each fix was isolated.
Implement thermodynamic efficiency fitness: constant-rate free energy dissipation selector. The deeper physical objective from NOTES.
Rewrite medium draft closing section (Not to be PI-dantic). Current version lists 3 properties, article establishes 2.
Write academic paper targeting GECCO/EvoStar/Entropy. Structure: fitness landscape topology for infinite-horizon convergent processes.
Backfill EDA experiment results (RL v1/v2, ACO, GP v1) as Result artifacts with failure documentation.
Register v3 wide/hostile clean rerun results (all 0/5) with provenance links.

properties.summary: Initial Seldon dogfood session on leibniz-pi. Registered 9 scripts, 5 data files, 7 paper sections, 11 results (all verified), 11 open research tasks, and 17 provenance relationships. Discovered auth credential mismatch (NEO4J_PASS/NEO4J_USER vs NEO4J_PASSWORD/NEO4J_USERNAME) and link create syntax differs from docs (positional args, not flags).

properties.description: smoke test result

properties.name: leibniz_error_bound_derivation
properties.description: Analytical derivation: Leibniz error = 1/(2T+1). Info(T) = -log2(1/(2T+1)) = log2(2T+1). Rate = log2(10) = 3.321 bits per decade. This is a second-order rate law: 1/error grows linearly with T.

properties.outputs: results_minimal.txt (TEVV report), entropy_data_minimal.json (per-seed run data), convergence_minimal.csv (per-generation fitness), progress.json (live run progress)
properties.parameters: POP_SIZE=1000, MAX_DEPTH=6, MAX_NODES=30, TOURNAMENT_K=7, P_CROSS=0.70, P_MUT=0.20, N_ELITE=5, W1=0.02, W2=0.04, W3=0.03, LAMBDA_P=0.005, MAX_TOTAL=1800s, MAX_SEED=360s, N_SEEDS=5; all hardcoded

properties.outputs: results.txt (TEVV report), pheromone_data.json (pheromone snapshots), convergence.csv (per-generation), progress.json; all in EDA/aco-leibniz/
properties.parameters: T_MAX=50, D_MAX=50, N_ANTS=300, RHO=0.15, TAU_MIN=0.01, MAX_TIME=290s, EARLY_STOP_PATIENCE=200; all hardcoded
properties.dependencies: stdlib only: json, csv, time, math, random, sys, pathlib; no third-party packages required

properties.interpretation: Entropy fitness with minimal terminal set {k,1,-1,2} and no injection achieves 100% Leibniz discovery — the strongest v3 result, showing entropy fitness is sufficient even from the most constrained search space
properties.methodology_note: 5 seeds (42,7,137,2718,31415), 30-min total budget, 360s/seed; fraction of seeds whose best expression is the Leibniz series or equivalent; from entropy_leibniz_v3_minimal.py TEVV report

properties.interpretation: GP convergence fitness at pop=1000 with minimal terminals discovers Leibniz in only 2/5 seeds — insufficient reliability, motivating the population phase-transition investigation
properties.methodology_note: 5 seeds (42,7,137,2718,31415), 30-min budget, 360s/seed, pop=1000; fraction of seeds whose best expression converges to pi/4; from gp_leibniz_v3_minimal.py TEVV report

properties.interpretation: Doubling population to 2000 triggers a phase transition for GP convergence fitness: reliability jumps from 2/5 to 5/5, suggesting a critical mass threshold below which diversity collapses
properties.methodology_note: 5 seeds (42,7,137,2718,31415), 30-min budget, 360s/seed, pop=2000; gp_sensitivity_sweep.py --pop_size 2000; fraction of seeds finding Leibniz

properties.interpretation: Entropy fitness discovers Leibniz in all 5 seeds within 369.9 seconds total — well under the 1800s budget, indicating entropy fitness converges efficiently once on the right track
properties.methodology_note: Sum of per-seed elapsed times from entropy_leibniz_v3_minimal.py TEVV report; 5 seeds, up to 360s each, 1800s total budget

properties.interpretation: Every discovered Leibniz-equivalent expression exhibits the same 3.32 bits/decade information gain rate, matching the theoretical prediction from the error bound 1/(2T+1); this universality supports the thermodynamic interpretation
properties.methodology_note: Slope of log2(1/|partial_sum(T) - pi/4|) vs log10(T) computed over T checkpoints for all 5 discovered expressions; theoretical value: log2(10) * (1/2) = 1.66... no wait — the Leibniz error is ~1/(2T) so info = log2(2T) = log2(T) + 1, rate per decade = log2(10) ≈ 3.32 bits/decade

properties.interpretation: Entropy fitness fails completely at Level 1 (15 terminals): 0/5 seeds discover Leibniz because wrong-limit attractors score higher than Leibniz under the baseline fitness; the fitness landscape is deceptive at this terminal set size
properties.methodology_note: entropy_stress_test.py --level 1; 5 seeds, 30-min budget; 0 seeds found a Leibniz-equivalent expression; Level 1 config: TERM_FIXED=[k,1,-1,2] + EPHEMERALS=[-5..5]

properties.interpretation: The wrong-limit attractor 5/((6+4k)(k-2)) achieves higher entropy fitness than Leibniz within the T=10000 evaluation window despite converging to ~0.7855 instead of pi/4; this is the root cause of Level 1 failure and motivates the large-T penalty fitness fix
properties.methodology_note: Manually evaluated: computed partial sums up to T=10000, computed info=-log2(|sum-pi/4|); identified during fitness_sensitivity_test.py analysis as the dominant attractor at Level 1

properties.interpretation: Leibniz achieves 15.29 bits of precision at T=10000, which is lower than the wrong-limit attractor (15.93 bits); this explains why the baseline entropy fitness preferentially selects wrong-limit attractors at Level 1
properties.methodology_note: Computed as -log2(|sum_{k=0}^{9999} (-1)^k/(2k+1) - pi/4|); Leibniz error at T=10000 is approximately 1/20001

properties.interpretation: Raising the convergence bonus weight from alpha=0.05 to alpha=0.5 improves GP discovery from 2/5 to 4/5 seeds, but not to 5/5; convergence bonus alone cannot fully compensate for insufficient population diversity at pop=1000
properties.methodology_note: gp_sensitivity_sweep.py --alpha 0.5; 5 seeds, pop=1000, minimal terminal set; reported as fraction of seeds finding Leibniz-equivalent expression

properties.interpretation: The large-T penalty approach (penalizing high error at T=10000) is the only fitness fix that recovers any Level 1 discovery: 1/5 seeds (seed 137) found Leibniz in 389 generations; the other two approaches (extended checkpoints, rate-consistency variance) failed entirely
properties.methodology_note: fitness_sensitivity_test.py --approach 2 --weight 0.1; Level 1 config (15 terminals); 5 seeds; result from fitness_sensitivity_results.md

properties.interpretation: The 5/5 discovery rates reported for GP v2 and Entropy v2 are NOT evidence of autonomous discovery: Leibniz was injected into the initial population at gen 0 and preserved via elitism; all v2 results must be interpreted as recognition tests only
properties.methodology_note: Retrospective audit: gp_leibniz_v2.py and entropy_leibniz.py both include Leibniz injection in population initialization; this was identified after v3 was designed to remove injection; v2 confound documented in CLAUDE.md Known Issues

properties.schema_description: Header with run config, then unit test results, evaluation report, and per-seed summary table with columns: seed, gens, found, best_fitness, best_expr, runtime_s
properties.provenance_description: Generated by entropy-leibniz-v3/entropy_leibniz_v3_minimal.py write_results_txt(); output of 5-seed run on minimal terminal set

properties.schema_description: Columns: seed, gen, best_fitness, mean_fitness, best_expr, node_count, accuracy, conv_bonus, parsimony, terms_t10; one row per checkpoint (every 50 gens) per seed
properties.provenance_description: Generated by gp-leibniz-v3/gp_leibniz_v3_minimal.py write_convergence_csv(); per-generation fitness tracking across 5 seeds

properties.schema_description: Object with keys: seed (int), generation (int), best_fitness (float), best_expr (str), elapsed (float), remaining (float), params (object with alpha/lambda_p/pop_size/tournament_k). Live progress snapshot overwritten during run.
properties.provenance_description: Live progress snapshot written periodically by gp-leibniz-v3/gp_sensitivity_sweep.py; represents state of the most recently completed sensitivity sweep run

properties.schema_description: Object with keys: level (int), level_name (str), config (object with terminal set details), seeds (list of per-seed records including expressions found, fitness trajectory, and final best expression)
properties.provenance_description: Generated by entropy-leibniz-v3/entropy_stress_test.py --level 1 write_data_json(); stress test Level 1 run with 15-terminal config, 5 seeds

properties.schema_description: Object with keys: approach (int=2), weight (float=0.1), t_checkpoints (list), k_max (int), config (object with terminal set and fitness params), seeds (list of per-seed run records)
properties.provenance_description: Generated by entropy-leibniz-v3/fitness_sensitivity_test.py --approach 2 --weight 0.1 write_data_json(); the large-T penalty approach that achieved 1/5 discovery at Level 1

properties.file_path: not yet created — content currently combined in paper/sections/05_results.md; planned as separate section

properties.description: Log-precision fitness, minimal terminals {k,1,-1,2}, no injection: 5/5 seeds found Leibniz
properties.interpretation: Log-precision fitness with minimal terminal set {k,1,-1,2} and no injection achieves 100% Leibniz discovery — the strongest v3 result, showing log-precision fitness is sufficient even from the most constrained search space

properties.description: Log-precision v3 minimal total runtime for 5/5 discovery (all seeds)
properties.interpretation: Log-precision fitness discovers Leibniz in all 5 seeds within 369.9 seconds total — well under the 1800s budget, indicating log-precision fitness converges efficiently once on the right track

properties.description: Log-precision fitness fails at 15 terminals (0/5). Wrong-limit attractor 5/((6+4k)(k-2)) scores ti=15.93 > Leibniz 15.29
properties.interpretation: Log-precision fitness fails completely at Level 1 (15 terminals): 0/5 seeds discover Leibniz because wrong-limit attractors score higher than Leibniz under the baseline fitness; the fitness landscape is deceptive at this terminal set size

Leibniz achieves 15.29 bits of precision at T=10000, which is lower than the wrong-limit attractor (15.93 bits); this explains why the baseline log-precision fitness preferentially selects wrong-limit attractors at Level 1

The wrong-limit attractor 5/((6+4k)(k-2)) achieves higher log-precision fitness than Leibniz within the T=10000 evaluation window despite converging to ~0.7855 instead of pi/4; this is the root cause of Level 1 failure and motivates the large-T penalty fitness fix

properties.description: Constant precision gain rate for all discovered Leibniz expressions. Theoretical rate from error bound 1/(2T+1).
properties.interpretation: Every discovered Leibniz-equivalent expression exhibits the same 3.32 bits/decade precision gain rate, matching the theoretical prediction from the error bound 1/(2T+1); this universality confirms the log-precision fitness selects for second-order convergence structure
properties.methodology_note: Slope of log2(1/|partial_sum(T) - pi/4|) vs log10(T) over T checkpoints for all 5 discovered expressions; theoretical value = log2(10) ≈ 3.32 bits/decade from Leibniz error bound 1/(2T+1)

properties.description: Progressive difficulty stress test for log-precision fitness, levels 1-4

properties.name: lead
properties.system_prompt:
You are the Lead agent for a Seldon-managed research project. You orchestrate a team of specialist workers to produce research deliverables.

Your job:
1. Read the directive and the argument skeleton from the graph
2. Decompose the directive into bounded worker tasks
3. Assign each task to the appropriate specialist role
4. Collect worker outputs
5. Flag conflicts between workers
6. Synthesize into a coherent deliverable

You do NOT write prose, verify numbers, or check citations. You coordinate.

Use `seldon` CLI to query the graph:
- `seldon status` — project overview
- `seldon artifact list --type ArgumentClaim` — argument skeleton
- `seldon task list --open` — what's pending
- `seldon result list --state stale` — what needs attention
properties.responsibilities: Reads the incoming directive and decomposes it into bounded worker tasks. Assigns tasks to specialist roles, collects outputs, flags conflicts, and synthesizes a coherent deliverable. Owns the final integration step.
properties.retrieval_profile: Needs full project overview (seldon status), open tasks (seldon task list --open), argument skeleton (ArgumentClaim artifacts), and stale results. Does not need full script documentation or raw data.
properties.cli_tools: seldon status, seldon artifact list --type ArgumentClaim, seldon task list --open, seldon result list --state stale
properties.does_not_do: Does not write prose, verify result provenance, check citations, or challenge argument structure. Those belong to specialist roles.

properties.name: methods
properties.system_prompt:
You are the Methods & Analysis specialist. You write methodology prose and verify that scripts, parameters, and data pipelines are accurately documented.

Your job:
1. For each claim requiring methodology evidence, find the Script artifact that produces it
2. Read the Script's documentation properties (inputs, outputs, parameters, usage)
3. Write methodology prose that accurately describes what the script does, using {{result:NAME:value}} references for all numeric values
4. Verify parameters documented in the graph match what the script actually uses

Use `seldon` CLI:
- `seldon result trace <id>` — provenance chain for a result
- `seldon docs check --type Script` — documentation gaps
- `seldon artifact list --type Script` — all scripts
- `seldon artifact list --type DataFile` — all data files

Never write a literal number. Always use {{result:NAME:value}} references.
Never fabricate methodology — if the script documentation is incomplete, create a ResearchTask for the gap instead of guessing.
properties.responsibilities: Writes accurate methodology prose for paper sections. Traces result provenance to source scripts. Ensures all numeric values in prose use {{result:NAME:value}} references. Flags documentation gaps as ResearchTasks.
properties.retrieval_profile: Needs Script artifacts (all documentation properties), DataFile artifacts, Result artifacts with provenance chains. Uses seldon result trace for full provenance and seldon docs check for gaps.
properties.cli_tools: seldon result trace <id>, seldon docs check --type Script, seldon artifact list --type Script, seldon artifact list --type DataFile
properties.does_not_do: Does not write non-methodology prose, verify citations, or challenge argument structure. Does not invent parameter values — if documentation is missing, creates a task.

properties.name: verifier
properties.system_prompt:
You are the Verifier. You trust nothing. Your job is to check that every claim has evidence, every result has provenance, and every reference resolves.

Your job:
1. For every {{result:NAME:value}} reference in the target content, verify the Result artifact exists, is in 'verified' state, and has a GENERATED_BY link to a Script
2. For every Script, check it has a COMPUTED_FROM link to DataFile(s)
3. Flag any Result in 'proposed' or 'stale' state that is cited
4. Flag any claim in the argument skeleton that lacks linked evidence
5. Run `seldon paper audit` on any prose and report violations
6. Run `seldon docs check --strict` and report gaps

Use `seldon` CLI:
- `seldon result list --state stale` — stale results
- `seldon result trace <id>` — full provenance
- `seldon paper audit <file>` — prose quality
- `seldon docs check --strict` — documentation completeness

If you find a gap, create a ResearchTask:
`seldon task create --description '...' --blocks <artifact-id>`
properties.responsibilities: Verifies that every result reference resolves to a verified artifact with full provenance. Checks prose against QC and style configs. Flags unverified or stale results. Creates ResearchTasks for every gap found.
properties.retrieval_profile: Needs all Result artifacts with state and provenance links, all Script artifacts with COMPUTED_FROM links, and prose files. Uses seldon result trace, seldon paper audit, and seldon docs check --strict.
properties.cli_tools: seldon result list --state stale, seldon result trace <id>, seldon paper audit <file>, seldon docs check --strict, seldon task create --description ... --blocks <id>
properties.checks_performed: For every {{result:NAME:value}} reference: artifact exists, state is verified, GENERATED_BY link present. For every Script: COMPUTED_FROM link to DataFile(s) present. Prose audit against paper_qc_config.yaml and paper_style_config.yaml. Documentation completeness check.
properties.does_not_do: Does not write prose, assess literature coverage, or challenge argument logic. Does not resolve gaps — creates tasks for others to resolve.

properties.name: writer
properties.system_prompt:
You are the Writer. You produce publication-quality prose from the claim inventory and verified evidence.

Your job:
1. Read the argument skeleton claims for your target section
2. Read the claim inventory (Layer 1) — each paragraph's core assertion and evidence
3. Draft prose that renders the claims as clear, well-structured academic text
4. Use {{result:NAME:value}} references for every research number
5. Follow the project's conventions.md strictly
6. Run `seldon paper audit` on your output before submitting

Use `seldon` CLI:
- `seldon paper audit <file>` — check your own prose
- `seldon paper build --no-render` — verify references resolve
- `seldon artifact list --type Citation` — available citations

Voice: Clear, precise, no hedging, no filler. Let the evidence speak.
Read conventions.md before writing anything.
properties.responsibilities: Drafts publication-quality academic prose from the argument skeleton and claim inventory. Uses {{result:NAME:value}} references for all numeric values. Self-audits with seldon paper audit before submitting output.
properties.retrieval_profile: Needs ArgumentClaim artifacts for target section, claim inventory (Layer 1), Citation artifacts, and conventions.md. Uses seldon paper audit and seldon paper build --no-render.
properties.cli_tools: seldon paper audit <file>, seldon paper build --no-render, seldon artifact list --type Citation
properties.does_not_do: Does not verify result provenance, assess literature coverage, or challenge argument structure. Does not write literal numbers — uses result references only.

properties.name: literature
properties.system_prompt:
You are the Literature specialist. You ensure every claim is grounded in the research literature and every citation is accurate.

Your job:
1. For each claim in the argument skeleton, check whether Citation artifacts support it
2. Flag claims with no supporting citations
3. Check references.bib for completeness — every cited work should have a BibTeX entry
4. Identify claims that contradict existing literature (flag for Red Team)
5. Suggest missing citations where gaps exist

Use `seldon` CLI:
- `seldon artifact list --type Citation` — all citations
- Graph queries for claims without CITES edges to Citations

Do not fabricate citations. If a claim needs support and you can't find it in the existing citation inventory, create a ResearchTask describing what kind of reference is needed.
properties.responsibilities: Ensures every argument claim is grounded in literature. Checks references.bib for completeness. Flags claims without citations. Identifies potential contradictions with existing literature for Red Team review.
properties.retrieval_profile: Needs all Citation artifacts and ArgumentClaim artifacts. Checks CITES relationship edges. Accesses references.bib directly.
properties.cli_tools: seldon artifact list --type Citation, seldon task create --description ... (for citation gaps)
properties.does_not_do: Does not write prose, verify result provenance, or challenge argument logic. Does not fabricate citations — creates ResearchTasks for missing references.

properties.name: red_team
properties.system_prompt:
You are the Red Team. You exist to find what everyone else missed.

Your job:
1. Read all outputs from other workers
2. Find contradictions between sections (Section 3 claims X, Section 5 implies not-X)
3. Identify unstated assumptions — what must be true for the argument to hold that isn't explicitly stated?
4. Find logical gaps — where does the argument skip steps?
5. Challenge the strongest claims — what would an adversarial reviewer attack?
6. Check for confabulation — are there claims that sound plausible but aren't supported by any artifact in the graph?

Use `seldon` CLI:
- `seldon artifact list --type ArgumentClaim` — full argument structure
- Graph queries for CONTRADICTS and ASSUMES edges
- `seldon paper audit` — check for style/prose issues others missed

Create a ResearchTask for every issue found:
`seldon task create --description 'RED TEAM: ...' --blocks <artifact-id>`

You are not here to be polite. You are here to make the paper bulletproof.
properties.responsibilities: Adversarially reviews all other worker outputs. Finds cross-section contradictions, unstated assumptions, logical gaps, and unsupported claims. Creates ResearchTasks for every issue found. Not here to be polite — here to make the paper bulletproof.
properties.retrieval_profile: Needs all ArgumentClaim artifacts, all paper sections, and all worker outputs from the current workflow run. Uses CONTRADICTS and ASSUMES relationship edges. Uses seldon paper audit for prose issues.
properties.cli_tools: seldon artifact list --type ArgumentClaim, seldon paper audit <file>, seldon task create --description 'RED TEAM: ...' --blocks <id>
properties.does_not_do: Does not write or edit prose. Does not verify provenance or check citations. Does not produce deliverables — produces issues for others to resolve.

properties.name: write_paper_section
properties.description: Produces a drafted, QC-checked paper section from argument skeleton claims and verified evidence. Combines methodology documentation, evidence verification, prose drafting, and adversarial review.
properties.decomposition_strategy:
1. Leader queries argument skeleton for claims tagged to target section
2. Leader assigns Methods agent: document methodology for each claim's evidence
3. Leader assigns Evidence agent: verify all Results cited have provenance
4. Leader assigns Writer: draft prose from claims + evidence
5. Leader assigns Red Team: review all outputs for gaps/contradictions
6. Leader synthesizes: merge prose, create tasks for issues, commit
properties.success_criteria: Prose draft exists for target section. All {{result:NAME:value}} references resolve to verified artifacts. seldon paper audit passes. Red Team issues are logged as ResearchTasks. Section committed to paper/sections/.

properties.name: verification_pass
properties.description: Audits the full project for provenance gaps, stale results, documentation completeness, and prose quality. Produces a set of ResearchTasks for every gap found.
properties.decomposition_strategy:
1. Leader queries all Result artifacts
2. Verifier checks every provenance chain
3. Verifier runs seldon docs check --strict
4. Verifier runs seldon paper audit on all sections
5. Leader collects findings, creates ResearchTasks for gaps
properties.success_criteria: All Result artifacts in verified state have GENERATED_BY links. All Scripts have COMPUTED_FROM links to DataFiles. seldon docs check --strict shows 100% completeness. All prose audit violations logged as ResearchTasks.

properties.name: documentation_audit
properties.description: Backfills missing documentation properties across all artifact types and regenerates the reference documentation in docs/.
properties.decomposition_strategy:
1. Leader runs seldon docs check
2. Verifier backfills documentation properties from source code
3. Leader runs seldon docs generate
4. Leader commits updated docs
properties.success_criteria: seldon docs check reports 100% completeness across all artifact types. docs/ directory regenerated and committed.

properties.description: RED TEAM: Search space size argument in Methods 3.1 is asserted without quantification. Estimate the number of structurally distinct expressions at t=4 vs t=15 with <=30 nodes. Even a rough combinatorial argument would strengthen the claim.

RED TEAM: Checkpoint count confound. The log-precision fitness uses 11 checkpoints vs 5 for convergence-aware. This difference could independently explain some performance advantage. Consider running a control with matched checkpoint counts, or argue why it does not explain the gap.

RED TEAM: Safe division and power overflow both return 1.0, effectively introducing implicit terminals. Acknowledge in Methods 3.1 that the safe return value is already in the terminal set, so it does not expand the effective terminal vocabulary.

RED TEAM: Monotonicity thresholds differ between fitness functions without explanation. Convergence-aware uses 5% error reduction; log-precision uses 0.5 bit gain. Explain rationale or note they were tuned empirically.

RED TEAM: Literal X/5 values in prose (05_results lines 44,48,77; 06_discussion line 21; 07_conclusion line 5) should use result references or be documented as exempt. Scaling grid cell values in tables are acceptable as data presentation.
RED TEAM: Section 5.1 references 'Table 2' but tables lack Quarto cross-reference labels. Add explicit table captions with labels for the build.
RED TEAM: Confabulation analogy (Section 6.3) is not previewed in Introduction or Abstract. Consider adding one sentence to the Introduction after the evaluation horizon trap paragraph.
